title: The Right Thing to Do
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 6-9-24 - AM- The Right Thing to Do - Acts 16_25-34 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Presbyterian Distinctives in Baptism
subpoints: Reformed Presbyterians differ from many evangelicals in three ways on baptism: (1) baptism is a sacrament through which God identifies Himself with the baptized and they join the visible church; (2) children of believers should be baptized; (3) the proper mode is pouring/sprinkling, not immersion
Occasion of the sermon: the baptism of covenant child Olivia
Christian baptism is mentioned only eleven times in the New Testament

outline.title: The Eleven New Testament Baptisms
subpoints: Acts 2: Jews baptized in response to Peter's Pentecost sermon
Acts 8: Samaritans baptized in response to Philip's preaching; the Ethiopian eunuch baptized after Philip explains Isaiah 53
Acts 9/22: Paul baptized after the direct preaching of Christ on the Damascus road
Acts 10: Cornelius's household baptized in response to Peter's preaching
Acts 16: Lydia's household baptized; the Philippian jailer's household baptized in response to Paul and Silas's singing and preaching
Acts 18:8: Corinthians baptized in response to Paul's preaching
Acts 19: John's disciples re-baptized with Christian baptism
1 Corinthians 1: Crispus and Gaius baptized in response to Paul's preaching
The one common thread: all baptisms were in response to the preaching of the gospel

outline.title: What Is a Household? The Four Household Baptisms
subpoints: A household in the Bible includes all related by blood or adoption who depend on a patriarch or matriarch, plus servants (Genesis 17:23)
The four household baptisms: Cornelius, Lydia, the Philippian jailer, and the household of Stephanas
In Acts 16:31-34, the word 'believed' (vv. 31, 34) is singular — referring to the jailer only; Luke goes out of his way not to mention the faith of the household members
These baptisms do not prove infants must be baptized, but they are consistent with infants being baptized; the Bible nowhere says babies must not be baptized
These baptisms show that God works through family units; children and servants were included without individual faith being mentioned

outline.title: Circumcision and Baptism Mean the Same Thing
subpoints: God's covenant with Abraham in Genesis 17 required circumcision as the sign of covenant membership for all males in the household
Moses was nearly killed by God for failing to circumcise his son (Exodus 4:24-26) — showing how important applying the sign was
Colossians 2:11-12: 'In Him you were also circumcised... buried with Him in baptism' — Paul equates circumcision and baptism; they mean the same thing
Galatians 3:26-29: Those baptized into Christ are Abraham's seed and heirs according to the promise — same family, different sign
In the New Covenant, women also receive the sign (not just males) because the New Covenant is a better, larger covenant; women stand in Christ directly, not through a male representative

The same types of people eligible for circumcision are eligible for baptism: adult converts from outside (believer's baptism) and children of believers from inside the covenant community (covenant baptism)

outline.title: Parental and Church Responsibilities
subpoints: Covenant baptism must not be held in isolation from the responsibility that accompanies it
Parents must raise their children to believe in Christ, obey His Word, and be faithful to God's covenant — Deuteronomy 6:1-6
Those who have children baptized out of tradition but do not raise them in the Christian faith are guilty of mocking God's ordinance
Baptism is the starting of Christian discipleship — it initiates children into the covenant community and into the church's responsibility
Parents who choose mediocre churches, neglect family worship, or miss public worship for frivolous reasons should not be surprised when children reject the faith
The whole church also has responsibility: elders equip parents; the congregation shows genuine interest; pastor, elders, and members pray for covenant children
The heart of the church is this: to bring the heart of the child to the heart of his Savior (William Hendriksen)

summary: Preached on the occasion of the baptism of covenant child Olivia, this sermon is a thorough treatment of the biblical and theological basis for covenant (infant) baptism in the context of Acts 16:25-34. Pastor Lyro begins by identifying three ways Reformed Presbyterians differ from many evangelicals on baptism: baptism is a sacrament, children of believers should receive it, and the proper mode is pouring or sprinkling. He surveys all eleven New Testament baptisms, noting the common thread — all were in response to the preaching of the gospel. He then examines the four household baptisms in detail, showing that the Greek words for 'believed' in the Philippian jailer passage are singular, meaning Luke attributes faith to the jailer alone while still recording that the whole household was baptized. He argues this is consistent with the inclusion of infants. The theological core of the sermon is the equivalence of circumcision and baptism demonstrated in Colossians 2:11-12 and Galatians 3:26-29: Paul equates the two signs, meaning the same types of people who received circumcision in the Old Covenant — adult converts and the children of covenant members — receive baptism in the New Covenant. Women also receive the New Covenant sign because the New Covenant is better, requiring no human representative. The sermon closes with an extended call to parental and congregational responsibility: covenant baptism must be accompanied by faithful parenting in an explicitly Christian home, and the whole church must come alongside covenant families.
transcript:
## Introduction: Three Presbyterian Distinctives on Baptism

A few years ago a member of our church told me she wished we were more like the other Christians out there. The comment was made in the context of tension in families because of our Reformed and Presbyterian beliefs and practices. And sometimes we feel the same way — we wish we were more like everybody else, we could all live in harmony. We look around and it seems like our Presbyterian beliefs and practices are a small part of a small part of a small minority in Christianity as a whole.

And one of the areas where we differ significantly from a large portion of Bible-believing American evangelicalism is in our doctrine of baptism. We differ from the average non-Presbyterian evangelical in three ways. First, we believe that the Bible teaches that baptism is a sacrament through which God identifies Himself with the one being baptized, and the one being baptized becomes part of the body of Christ, which is in this case the visible church in its local representation. You are not part of the church of Jesus Christ if you are not baptized. Second, we believe that because of this, those who confess Christ and their children should be baptized. God is a God to us and to our children; His covenant promises require us to baptize our children on the strength of God's promises to families and on the strength of the faith of the parents. Third, we believe the proper mode — the way baptism should be done — is by the pouring and sprinkling of water on the head of the one being baptized.

As we prepare to baptize little Olivia, I want us to consider the rightness, the duty, the privilege, and the blessing of baptizing a covenant child. Christian baptism is only mentioned eleven times in the New Testament.

## The Eleven New Testament Baptisms

In Acts 2, Jews are baptized in response to Peter's Pentecost preaching — convicted of their sins, they ask what to do, and Peter says repent and be baptized. In Acts 8, the Samaritans are baptized in response to Philip's preaching; the Ethiopian eunuch is baptized after Philip explains Isaiah 53 to him. The Ethiopian is the pastor's dream encounter — the man is reading Isaiah 53 and wants to understand it, and Philip points to Christ from Isaiah 53 and baptizes him. In Acts 9/22, Paul is baptized after Christ appears to him on the Damascus road. In Acts 10, Cornelius's household is baptized in response to Peter's preaching. In Acts 16, Lydia's house is baptized, and the Philippian jailer's household is baptized in response to Paul and Silas's singing and preaching. In Acts 18:8, the Corinthians are baptized in response to Paul's preaching. In Acts 19, a group of John's disciples who had only John's baptism are baptized with Christian baptism after Paul preaches to them. In 1 Corinthians 1, Crispus and Gaius are mentioned as having been baptized in response to Paul's preaching.

If you are paying attention: what is the one common thread in all of these? The preaching. It was always in response to the preaching of the gospel.

## What Is a Household?

Four of these eleven are household baptisms. To understand them, we first need to understand what a household is. Genesis 17:23 tells us that a household in the Bible included all who were related by blood or adoption who were dependent on a patriarch or matriarch. A household also included servants. So when Luke and Paul say so-and-so's household was baptized, they mean every class of people belonging to that household — old and young, blood relatives and slaves.

These four household baptisms tell us at least three things. First, there is no reference to the faith of the household members in three of the four cases. In the case of the Philippian jailer in Acts 16:31-34, Luke seems to go out of his way not to mention the faith of the rest of the household. In verse 31, the word "believed" is singular — referring to one person — despite the fact that members of his household are present. In verse 34, "having believed" is again singular, referring to the Philippian jailer only. Luke says nothing at all about the family's faith, though it would have been easy for him to do so.

Second, it is possible that young children and infants were part of these households. At the very least, these baptisms are consistent with children and infants being baptized. These baptisms do not prove that infants and children are to be baptized. Does the Bible say anywhere that babies must not be baptized? It does not.

Third, these baptisms show that God works through families — He is looking at family units as He deals with His people in the book of Acts.

## Circumcision and Baptism: The Same Sign

Baptism is not explicitly commanded for infants anywhere, but because something is not explicitly commanded does not mean it is not commanded. We can see what is true through the explicit teaching of the Bible or by good and necessary consequence from the clear teaching of the Bible.

The Bible teaches that children of believers should be baptized by equating the significance of baptism with the meaning of circumcision. In the Old Testament, children of believers were part of the people of God — part of the covenant community — throughout the Old Testament. That is not debated. And the prophecies of the Old Testament regarding the people of God include children. Joel 2:15-16 says, in calling the sacred assembly: "Gather the people, sanctify the congregation, assemble the elders, gather the children and nursing babes." Nursing babes are part of the sacred assembly.

God's covenant with Abraham in Genesis 17:9-14: "God said to Abraham, as for you, you shall keep my covenant, you and your descendants after you throughout their generations. This is my covenant which you shall keep, between me and you and your descendants after you: every male child among you shall be circumcised... And the uncircumcised male child who is not circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin, that person shall be cut off from his people; he has broken my covenant." The sign of being part of God's covenant people was circumcision.

The importance of applying this sign to children is illustrated in the life of Moses. Moses was so busy for God that he failed to circumcise his son. In Exodus 4:24-26, God sends an angel of death to kill Moses. His wife Zipporah, realizing what was happening, circumcised the boy on the spot. The point: it was important enough to God that He was going to discipline Moses with death for not circumcising his son.

Now turn to Colossians 2:11-12. It says: "In Him you were also circumcised with the circumcision made without hands, by putting off the body of the sins of the flesh, by the circumcision of Christ, buried with Him in baptism." Note that "you were circumcised" is the main finite verb of the sentence — that is the main action. "Buried with Him in baptism" is a participle explaining how the main verb was accomplished. You were circumcised — how? By having been buried with Him in baptism. Paul ties together circumcision and baptism: they mean the same thing, accomplish the same thing.

Galatians 3:26-29: "For you are all sons of God through faith in Christ Jesus. For as many of you as were baptized into Christ have put on Christ. There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free, there is neither male nor female; for you are all one in Christ Jesus. And if you are Christ's, then you are Abraham's seed and heirs according to the promise." Genesis 17 said you are Abraham's family through circumcision; Galatians 3 says you are Abraham's family through baptism. Paul already told us in Colossians 2 that they mean the same thing.

That being the case, the same types of people who should have been circumcised in the Old Testament are eligible for baptism in the New Testament. Believers' circumcision, or believers' baptism, for those coming from outside the visible church. Covenant circumcision, or covenant baptism, for those coming from inside the visible church on the strength of the parents' profession of faith — just like children and family members were circumcised in the Old Testament.

But wait — in the Old Testament, only boys were circumcised, and we are about to baptize Olivia. The answer: the New Covenant is a better covenant. It is a bigger covenant. Women stand in it through Christ directly, not through the representation of their father or husband. Therefore not only males receive the sign of the covenant — females also receive the sign, because it is a better covenant.

## Parental and Church Responsibilities

The doctrine of covenant baptism must not be held in isolation from the responsibility that accompanies it. Baptism carries certain obligations and duties on the part of Christian parents and the church as a whole. This is not Gabe and Alia and Olivia by themselves. This is Gabe and Alia before God, with all of us come alongside them.

Since baptized children are members of the visible church and part of the covenant community, parents must raise their children to believe in Christ, to obey His Word, and to be faithful to God's covenant. Gabe and Alia — the truth is that you must love Christ most, more than you love Olivia. For you to be the best parents you can be, you must love Christ above love for her. That is the best kind of love you can give to her as well.

People who have their children baptized because of tradition and do not raise their children in the Christian faith are guilty of mocking God's ordinance, treating baptism as a magic superstition. Baptism is the starting of Christian discipleship. In the Great Commission, Christ says: make disciples of all the nations by baptizing them in the name of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — initiating them in discipleship.

The doctrine of covenant headship and the wonderful promises of God related to the children of believers presuppose that covenant children do not automatically become faithful Christians. Parents need to be instruments in God's hands to push their children toward Christ. Parents have the sobering responsibility to live the faith before their children and diligently teach them the whole counsel of God. When parents are bad examples, neglect family worship, choose to attend a mediocre church, miss public worship for frivolous reasons, or are not careful about the education of their children — they should not be surprised when their children reject the faith.

Deuteronomy 6:1-6: "You shall fear the Lord your God, to keep all His statutes and His commandments... You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, and with all your strength. And these words which I command you today shall be in your heart." Baptized children need to grow up in explicitly Christian homes where Christ is first in all things.

The church as a whole also has responsibilities: the elders are to provide leadership and training so that parents have the tools to raise a godly family; the congregation is to show genuine interest in the development of covenant children; the pastor, elders, and all church members are to pray for covenant children and encourage them and their parents. We must not neglect the church's responsibility to these members of the covenant. Children become members of this church when they are baptized — not when they later profess faith. They are members now.

The heart of the Christian church is this: to bring the heart of the child to the heart of his Savior. And that is what you are doing in parenting. It is your duty before God, Gabe and Alia, to do whatever you can to influence your child in the gospel. One of the foolish things parents can believe is that they should let their children decide for themselves who they are and what they believe — because everyone else in the world is going to be trying to convince your child of whom he or she is and what they should believe. So be instruments in God's hand, entirely devoted to your children, because the very heart of Christian church is this: to bring the heart of the child to the heart of his Savior.

Father in heaven, we thank you that you are a good God. We thank you that you minister to us through your word, and that you minister to families as your people. We pray that these words proclaimed this morning would find a place in our hearts, that we would meditate upon them and grow in you through them. In Jesus' name. Amen.
